exercises.title: Process a Product Catalog
exercises.description: Given a list of product dictionaries, print all in-stock items sorted by price, and calculate the total inventory value.
exercises.starterCode:
catalog = [
    {"sku": "PY001", "name": "Python Book", "price": 29.99, "stock": 50},
    {"sku": "PY002", "name": "USB Keyboard", "price": 49.99, "stock": 0},
    {"sku": "PY003", "name": "Coffee Mug", "price": 12.99, "stock": 100},
    {"sku": "PY004", "name": "Desk Lamp", "price": 34.99, "stock": 25},
]

# TODO: Print the names of in-stock items (stock > 0) sorted by price
# Format: "$12.99 - Coffee Mug"

# TODO: Calculate and print the total inventory value (price * stock for all items)
# Format: "Total inventory value: $4949.75"
exercises.expectedOutput:
$12.99 - Coffee Mug
$29.99 - Python Book
$34.99 - Desk Lamp
Total inventory value: $4949.75
hints: Filter with [p for p in catalog if p['stock'] > 0]
Sort with sorted(..., key=lambda p: p['price'])
Total value: sum(p['price'] * p['stock'] for p in catalog)
Use f"${value:.2f}" to format as currency

exercises.title: Navigate a Nested User Profile
exercises.description: Access deeply nested data from a user profile dict. Use safe .get() chaining to retrieve nested values and handle missing keys gracefully.
exercises.starterCode:
user = {
    "name": "Alice",
    "age": 28,
    "preferences": {"theme": "dark", "notifications": True},
    "roles": ["admin", "editor"],
    "address": None,
}

# TODO: Print the user's name

# TODO: Print the user's theme preference

# TODO: Print the user's first role

# TODO: Use safe .get() chaining to print the user's city
# (address is None, so use .get() with default 'Unknown')

# TODO: Print whether notifications are enabled (True/False)
hints: user.get('address') or {} then .get('city', 'Unknown') handles None safely

exercises.title: Aggregate Order Data
exercises.description: Given a list of order dicts, calculate: total revenue, the order with the most items, and total items per customer.
exercises.starterCode:
orders = [
    {"order_id": "O1", "customer": "Alice", "items": [{"name": "Book", "qty": 2, "price": 29.99}, {"name": "Mug", "qty": 1, "price": 12.99}]},
    {"order_id": "O2", "customer": "Bob", "items": [{"name": "Lamp", "qty": 1, "price": 34.99}]},
    {"order_id": "O3", "customer": "Alice", "items": [{"name": "Keyboard", "qty": 1, "price": 49.99}, {"name": "Book", "qty": 3, "price": 29.99}]},
]

# TODO: Calculate and print total revenue across all orders
# Revenue per item = qty * price
# Format: "Total revenue: $251.91"

# TODO: Find and print the order_id with the most total items (sum of all qtys)
# Format: "Largest order: O3"

# TODO: Print total items ordered per customer
# Format: "Alice: 7\nBob: 1"
exercises.expectedOutput:
Total revenue: $251.91
Largest order: O3
Alice: 7
Bob: 1
hints: sum(item['qty'] * item['price'] for order in orders for item in order['items'])
For each order, sum item quantities: sum(i['qty'] for i in order['items'])
Use a dict to accumulate per-customer totals

warmup.title: Quick Recall: Chained Indexing
warmup.description: What does this code print? Trace each access step by step.
